past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 12 years ago, well-controlled on medication
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 8 years ago, controlled with oral agents
Mild osteoarthritis of the lumbar spine
family_history: Father had essential tremor diagnosed in his 70s
Mother had hypertension and type 2 diabetes, died of stroke at age 79
One brother, age 72, has hypertension and hyperlipidemia
No known family history of Parkinson disease or other neurodegenerative conditions

social_history.education: High school diploma and trade certification
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a two-story home in suburban neighborhood
social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, quit 20 years ago, 10 pack-year history
social_history.exercise: Used to walk 2 miles daily, has reduced activity over the past year due to stiffness and balance concerns

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and fully oriented to person, place, time, and situation. MMSE 28/30 (lost 1 point on recall at 5 minutes, 1 point on serial sevens). Speech is hypophonic with slightly monotone prosody but fluent and grammatically correct. Naming, repetition, and comprehension intact. Clock drawing normal.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial expression is notably decreased bilaterally (masked facies), more prominent on the right. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone is increased in the right upper extremity with cogwheel rigidity at the wrist, and mild lead-pipe rigidity at the right elbow. Left upper extremity shows mild cogwheel rigidity at the wrist. Lower extremities show mildly increased tone bilaterally, right greater than left. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. No pronator drift. Right hand resting tremor at 4-5 Hz, pill-rolling in character, diminishes with action. Mild intermittent left hand resting tremor noted when patient is distracted during mental tasks.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, temperature, vibration, and proprioception in all extremities.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric in biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles tendons bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No Hoffmann sign.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing normal bilaterally. Heel-to-shin testing normal bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements are slow and decremental on the right, with progressive reduction in amplitude (bradykinesia). Left hand shows subtle decrement on repetitive finger tapping.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait is slow with reduced right arm swing. Stride length is mildly shortened. Mild forward flexion of the trunk (stooped posture). Mild difficulty with turns, requiring 3-4 steps to complete a 180-degree turn (en bloc turning). Pull test shows 1 step retropulsion but self-corrects. No festination. Tandem gait is mildly impaired.
neurological_exam.additional: Glabellar tap reflex positive (Myerson sign). Seborrhea noted on the forehead and nasolabial folds. Micrographia demonstrated when asked to write a sentence. No myoclonus. No apraxia. No alien limb phenomenon.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive right hand tremor and stiffness for 18 months, with worsening difficulty walking
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Crystal Morris is a 62-year-old right-handed retired postal worker who presents to the neurology outpatient clinic accompanied by her wife, Maria, for evaluation of a progressive right hand tremor and generalized slowing of movement over the past 18 months. Her wife first noticed the tremor in her right hand approximately a year and a half ago while they were watching television in the evening. She observed her right hand shaking while it rested on her lap, and it would stop when she reached for the remote control. Over the subsequent months, the tremor has become more persistent and now occurs throughout the day, though it remains most prominent at rest. Her wife also reports that her handwriting has become noticeably smaller and more cramped over the past year, to the point where she can barely read her grocery lists anymore. Ms. Delgado herself has noticed that buttoning her shirts has become increasingly difficult, especially with her right hand, and shaving takes much longer than it used to. She describes a pervasive sense of stiffness in her right arm and shoulder that she initially attributed to arthritis. Over the past six to eight months, her wife has noticed that she walks more slowly with a slight shuffle and that her right arm no longer swings naturally when she walks. She had one near-fall two months ago when she turned quickly to answer the telephone and lost her balance briefly, catching herself on the kitchen counter. She has not had any frank falls. Her wife has also noted that her facial expression seems less animated than before, and her voice has become quieter. She reports mild constipation over the past year and occasional difficulty staying asleep, though she denies vivid dreams or acting out in her sleep. She denies any cognitive difficulties beyond occasional word-finding pauses, which her wife confirms are no different from baseline. She has not experienced visual hallucinations, orthostatic lightheadedness, or urinary incontinence. Her mood has been slightly subdued, but she denies frank depression or anxiety.

signal_characteristics.T1: Mild generalized cortical volume loss appropriate for age

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Scattered punctate periventricular and subcortical white matter hyperintensities, consistent with chronic microvascular change. No confluent lesions.

volumetrics.ventricular_system: Normal ventricular size, no ventriculomegaly disproportionate to sulcal prominence
volumetrics.substantia_nigra: No focal signal abnormality in the midbrain or substantia nigra on susceptibility-weighted imaging

additional_observations: No acute infarcts on DWI sequences
No basal ganglia infarcts or lacunes
No evidence of hemorrhage on SWI
No hot cross bun sign in the pons
No midbrain atrophy or hummingbird sign
Patent major intracranial vessels on MRA
mri.impression: Normal brain MRI for age. Mild age-appropriate cortical atrophy and Fazekas grade 1 periventricular white matter changes. No structural cause for parkinsonism identified. No features of normal pressure hydrocephalus (no ventriculomegaly disproportionate to sulcal atrophy). No basal ganglia infarcts to suggest vascular parkinsonism. No midbrain or cerebellar atrophy to suggest atypical parkinsonian syndromes. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Chloride 113 mEq/L (H), Glucose 122 mg/dL (H), HbA1c 6.9 % (H).

findings.region: Bilateral putamina, right greater than left
findings.signal: DaTscan_SPECT: Asymmetric reduction in dopamine transporter binding in bilateral putamina, with more pronounced reduction on the right (contralateral to the clinically more affected side). Right putamen shows comma-shaped to period-shaped loss of uptake. Left putamen shows early comma-shaped reduction in posterior aspect.; Caudate: Caudate nuclei show relatively preserved uptake bilaterally

output.impression: ABNORMAL DaTscan. Asymmetric bilateral reduction in striatal dopamine transporter uptake, right greater than left, with preferential involvement of the posterior putamina. The asymmetry correlates with the patient's clinically more affected left body side (contralateral to right putaminal deficit). Clinical correlation recommended.

Levodopa_Challenge_Test.test: Post-Levodopa UPDRS-III Motor Score (at 60 minutes after 200 mg levodopa/50 mg carbidopa)

findings.test: Head-Up Tilt Table - Standing BP (3 minutes)
Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Immediate Recall
Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Delayed Recall

output.impression: Abnormal values: Geriatric Depression Scale 6/15 points (H).
Abnormal values: Sleep Efficiency 82% % (H), REM Sleep Percentage 18% % (H), REM Sleep Without Atonia (RSWA) Present  (H).

output.query: MDS clinical diagnostic criteria Parkinson disease

results.title: MDS Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Parkinson's Disease
results.authors: Postuma RB, Berg D, Stern M, et al.
results.summary: The MDS criteria require parkinsonism (bradykinesia plus rest tremor or rigidity) as an entry criterion, then apply supportive criteria, absolute exclusion criteria, and red flags. Clinically established PD requires at least 2 supportive criteria and no exclusion criteria or red flags.

results.title: Diagnosis and treatment of Parkinson disease: A review
results.summary: Comprehensive review covering clinical features, diagnostic approach, and evidence-based treatment of Parkinson disease. Emphasizes that PD remains a clinical diagnosis based on motor features, with DaTscan useful in uncertain cases.

output.summary: The MDS Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Parkinson disease (Postuma et al., 2015) define parkinsonism as bradykinesia combined with rest tremor, rigidity, or both. For clinically established PD, at least two supportive criteria are needed (clear beneficial response to dopaminergic therapy, levodopa-induced dyskinesias, rest tremor of a limb, positive DaTscan or cardiac MIBG) with absence of absolute exclusion criteria and red flags. This patient meets the entry criterion (bradykinesia plus resting tremor and rigidity) and has supportive criteria including resting tremor and a positive levodopa response.

interactions: No clinically significant interactions with amlodipine, metformin, oxybutynin, or aspirin
contraindications: Do not use concurrently with non-selective MAO inhibitors (risk of hypertensive crisis)
warnings: May cause orthostatic hypotension, particularly in combination with amlodipine; monitor blood pressure
Nausea is common at initiation; take with a small snack if needed but avoid high-protein meals which can reduce absorption
May cause drowsiness and sudden sleep onset; counsel regarding driving safety
Iron supplements and metformin may reduce levodopa absorption; separate dosing by 2 hours if iron is added
Monitor for impulse control disorders (pathological gambling, hypersexuality, compulsive shopping)
alternatives: Pramipexole 0.125-1.5 mg TID (dopamine agonist, may be considered in younger patients)
Rasagiline 1 mg daily (MAO-B inhibitor, may be used as monotherapy in mild disease)

differential.key_distinguishing: Tremor in this patient is a classic resting tremor with pill-rolling quality that diminishes with action, unlike action/postural tremor of essential tremor. Additionally, this patient has bradykinesia and rigidity, which are not features of essential tremor.

The patient is not on any dopamine-blocking medications. No exposure to antipsychotics, metoclopramide, or other offending agents. Additionally, the asymmetric presentation favors idiopathic PD over drug-induced parkinsonism, which is typically symmetric.

No early or prominent autonomic failure (no orthostatic hypotension, no urinary incontinence). No cerebellar signs. Normal autonomic reflex testing. Good response to levodopa challenge. MRI shows no pontine or cerebellar atrophy.
No vertical gaze palsy, no early falls, no axial rigidity predominance. Tremor is prominent, which is atypical for PSP. No midbrain atrophy or hummingbird sign on MRI.

No urinary incontinence, no cognitive impairment consistent with NPH triad. No ventriculomegaly disproportionate to sulcal atrophy on MRI. Gait pattern is parkinsonian (shuffling, reduced arm swing) rather than magnetic/apractic gait of NPH.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history from patient and informant, including onset, progression, laterality of symptoms, functional impact, medication review, and screening for autonomic dysfunction, sleep disturbance, mood changes, and cognitive complaints
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Progressive asymmetric resting tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, and gait disturbance over 18 months with right-sided predominance; no red flags for atypical parkinsonism

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination with focus on movement disorder assessment including UPDRS-III motor examination
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Asymmetric right-sided resting tremor, cogwheel rigidity, bradykinesia with decrement on rapid alternating movements, shuffling gait with reduced arm swing, masked facies, and hypophonia

optimal_actions.action: Order screening labs to exclude secondary causes of parkinsonism: CBC, BMP, TSH, vitamin B12, ceruloplasmin, RPR
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All within normal limits except for known diabetes-related glucose and HbA1c elevations; no secondary cause identified

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to exclude structural causes of parkinsonism and evaluate for features of atypical parkinsonian syndromes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal brain MRI for age; no basal ganglia infarcts, no ventriculomegaly, no midbrain or cerebellar atrophy

optimal_actions.action: Initiate levodopa therapy with carbidopa-levodopa 25/100 mg three times daily and monitor response
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No significant drug interactions; positive clinical response expected and supports diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Consider DaTscan if diagnostic uncertainty persists after clinical evaluation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Asymmetric reduction in striatal dopamine transporter uptake, confirming presynaptic dopaminergic deficit

optimal_actions.action: Screen for non-motor symptoms: depression (GDS or PHQ-9), sleep disturbance (RBD screening questionnaire), constipation, and orthostatic hypotension
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild depressive symptoms, possible subclinical RBD, constipation reported

optimal_actions.action: Refer to physical therapy for gait training, balance exercises, and fall prevention, and to occupational therapy for fine motor strategies
Counsel patient and family on diagnosis, disease course, treatment expectations, driving safety, and exercise recommendations
Schedule follow-up in 4-6 weeks to assess levodopa response and titrate dose as needed

critical_actions: Identify the cardinal motor features of parkinsonism (bradykinesia, resting tremor, rigidity) with asymmetric onset
Review medication list to exclude drug-induced parkinsonism
Obtain brain MRI to exclude structural causes (vascular parkinsonism, NPH) and features of atypical parkinsonian syndromes
Order screening labs to exclude metabolic and treatable causes of parkinsonism (TSH, B12, ceruloplasmin)
Initiate levodopa therapy and assess for clinical response
Screen for non-motor symptoms including depression, sleep disturbance, and autonomic dysfunction
Refer to physical and occupational therapy
contraindicated_actions: Prescribing dopamine-blocking antiemetics (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine) which worsen parkinsonism
Prescribing typical antipsychotics (haloperidol) which block dopamine receptors and exacerbate symptoms
Abrupt discontinuation of dopaminergic therapy once started (risk of neuroleptic malignant-like syndrome)
Prescribing anticholinergic medications as first-line treatment in an elderly patient (risk of cognitive side effects and delirium)
Prescribing non-selective MAO inhibitors concurrently with levodopa (risk of hypertensive crisis)
Ordering brain biopsy (not indicated; PD is a clinical diagnosis)
key_reasoning_points: The asymmetric resting tremor with pill-rolling character, cogwheel rigidity, and bradykinesia with decrement on rapid alternating movements constitute the classic motor triad of Parkinson disease
Right-sided predominance of motor signs is typical of PD, which characteristically presents asymmetrically
Associated features including masked facies, hypophonia, micrographia, stooped posture, reduced arm swing, and en bloc turning further support the diagnosis
Normal brain MRI is expected in idiopathic PD and importantly excludes structural causes such as vascular parkinsonism, NPH, and atypical parkinsonian syndromes
Mild constipation and possible subclinical RBD on polysomnography represent non-motor features of alpha-synucleinopathy that support the PD diagnosis
Absence of red flags (early falls, vertical gaze palsy, prominent autonomic failure, cerebellar signs, symmetric onset, poor levodopa response) argues against atypical parkinsonism
A robust 50% improvement on levodopa challenge test meets the MDS supportive criterion for clinically established PD
The father's essential tremor in his 70s is a relevant but distinct condition that should not be confused with the patient's resting tremor presentation

key_educational_points: Parkinson disease is diagnosed clinically based on the presence of bradykinesia plus resting tremor or rigidity
Asymmetric onset is a hallmark of idiopathic PD and helps distinguish it from drug-induced and vascular parkinsonism
Brain MRI is typically normal in PD but is important to exclude structural causes and features of atypical parkinsonism
DaTscan confirms presynaptic dopaminergic deficit but does not distinguish PD from atypical parkinsonian syndromes
A positive levodopa response is a supportive diagnostic criterion and guides treatment
